question: If I receive a gift from a colleague at another central bank, do I need to register it with anyone?
answer: Advantages offered by other central banks, national public bodies, or European and international organisations may be accepted provided they do not go beyond what is customary and considered appropriate, and that they are neither frequent nor from the same source. (ECB Staff Rules, page 6)

question: What is the maximum value of hospitality from the private sector that I can accept during a work-related occasion?
answer: Hospitality offered by the private sector may be accepted up to a value of EUR 50 if it is offered in the context of a work-related occasion, provided it is not frequent and not from the same source. This exception does not apply to hospitality offered by current or potential suppliers or by credit institutions in the context of on-site inspections or audits, where no hospitality may be accepted. (ECB Staff Rules, page 6)

question: How long is the cooling-off period for staff at salary band I or above who were involved in supervising a credit institution before they can join that institution?
answer: Members of staff at salary band I or above who were directly involved in the supervision of a credit institution may only start working for that credit institution after the expiry of one year from the date on which their direct involvement in the supervision ceased. In exceptional circumstances, this period may be increased up to two years. (ECB Staff Rules, page 10)

question: I want to engage in unpaid volunteering work at a charity unrelated to the ECB. Do I need authorisation?
answer: No authorisation is required for external activities that are unremunerated, in the domain of culture, science, education, sport, charity, religion, social or other benevolent work, and not related to the ECB or the member of staff’s professional duties at the ECB. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 7–8)

question: When making a request for prior authorisation for a private financial transaction, how far in advance do I need to submit it?
answer: Any such request for authorisation must be submitted to the Compliance and Governance Office at least five working days prior to the envisaged order date in the format specified by that Office. If the Compliance and Governance Office does not react within five working days, the transaction shall be deemed to be authorised. (ECB Staff Rules, page 17)

question: What is the threshold above which a foreign exchange transaction (other than for personal travel or non-financial purposes) requires prior authorisation?
answer: Foreign exchange transactions other than those connected with the occasional acquisition of non-financial investments, private travel, or current and future personal expenses in another currency require prior authorisation from the Compliance and Governance Office when they exceed EUR 10,000 within any given calendar month. (ECB Staff Rules, page 15)

question: Within what timeframe must a whistleblower submit a request for protection against retaliation?
answer: The request for protection from retaliation shall be submitted to the Chief Compliance and Governance Officer within 24 months from the time of the occurrence of the act or omission alleged to be retaliation, together with any relevant documents or information supporting the request. (ECB Staff Rules, page 27)

question: How long is my personal file kept after my employment with the ECB ends?
answer: Provided that there are no pending claims, the personal file shall be retained for a maximum of 10 years. The retention period commences either when the staff member’s employment with the ECB ceases or following the last ECB pension payment to the pensioner or their entitled dependants. (ECB Staff Rules, page 31)

question: How much advance notice must be given to the Executive Board before staff can go on strike?
answer: The organising body shall inform the Executive Board in writing of the intention to strike at least ten working days before the first day of the strike. The written notice shall state the precise nature of the dispute, the precise nature of the proposed strike action, and the period during which the strike is going to take place. (ECB Staff Rules, page 31)

question: What languages must I have knowledge of to be eligible for appointment at the ECB?
answer: Candidates must have a thorough knowledge of English and a satisfactory knowledge of at least a second official Union language, unless an exception is authorised prior to the publication of the vacancy notice by the appointing authority. (ECB Staff Rules, page 33)

question: How long is a list of suitable candidates from a selection procedure valid?
answer: The list of suitable candidates shall be valid for a period of two years from the date it is drawn up by the appointing authority. (ECB Staff Rules, page 40)

question: What is the duration of the probationary period for new appointments?
answer: Appointments are subject to a probationary period of six months unless the Chief Services Officer, on behalf of the Executive Board, decides to waive it. In exceptional circumstances, the Chief Services Officer may determine a probationary period longer than six months, which may in any case be extended up to a maximum of twelve months. (ECB Staff Rules, page 47)

question: What is the confirmation period for an appointment to a managerial position at a higher salary band?
answer: The confirmation period shall be six months. Where the member of staff is prevented by illness, accident, maternity or, in exceptional circumstances, special leave from performing their duties for a period of more than one month, the appointing authority may extend the confirmation period accordingly. (ECB Staff Rules, page 43)

question: What is the reference period used for the regular performance management and development process?
answer: The reference period for the regular performance management and development shall run from 1 September of each year until 31 August of the following year. When a reviewee’s employment with the ECB starts after 1 September, the reference period shall begin on the first day of employment and end on the next 31 August. (ECB Staff Rules, page 49)

question: Up to what age is a child considered a dependent child for the purposes of the child allowance?
answer: To qualify as a dependent child, the child must be under 26 years of age, must reside in the member of staff’s household or be financially supported by the member of staff, must not receive a gross annual income above the prescribed threshold in the current calendar year, must not be on military or alternative service, and must not be married or in a registered partnership. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 63–64)

question: Until when is the pre-school allowance paid?
answer: The pre-school allowance commences in the month in which the dependent child is born and is paid until the first day of the month in which the child begins to attend a primary educational establishment, and in any event no later than the last day of the month in which the child reaches the age of seven. (ECB Staff Rules, page 69)

question: What is the standard subsistence allowance amount and for how long can I receive it?
answer: Members of staff entitled to the subsistence allowance for the entire eligible period are entitled to a standard subsistence allowance of EUR 9,270, increased by a maximum supplement of EUR 2,070 for any spouse, recognised partner and dependent child accompanying them. The maximum period of entitlement is three months and accrues on a daily basis. (ECB Staff Rules, page 74)

question: On which day of the month is salary paid?
answer: Members of staff are paid on the 15th of each month unless this date falls on a weekend or a public holiday observed by the ECB, in which case payment is made on the nearest working day immediately before this date. Payments are effected by direct credit to a SEPA-compliant bank account. (ECB Staff Rules, page 61)

question: What is the mileage rate paid for daily local transportation between a child's home and school under the education allowance?
answer: Provided there is a distance of more than one kilometre between the home closest to the school and the educational establishment attended, the rate paid shall be EUR 0.40 per kilometre. The mileage fee is paid monthly using the formula (km × 2 × 0.40 EUR × 180 school days) ÷ 12. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 67–68)

question: What are the core time periods I must be present at the workplace under the flexitime arrangement?
answer: The core time periods are Monday from 2 p.m. to 4.30 p.m.; Tuesday to Thursday from 10 a.m. to 12 p.m. and from 2 p.m. to 4.30 p.m.; and Friday from 10 a.m. to 12 p.m. (ECB Staff Rules, page 78)

question: What is the maximum number of teleworking days I can take per calendar year, and how many of those can be from outside the EU?
answer: Members of staff may telework for a maximum of 110 working days per calendar year from their registered place of residence in the country of their place of employment. Within those 110 days, up to 90 days may be from a location within the Union, and within those 90 days, up to 20 working days may be from a location outside the Union. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 83–84)

question: What is the maximum number of consecutive working days I can telework?
answer: Teleworking shall not exceed a maximum of 10 consecutive working days. This limit applies both within and across calendar months, and also applies where teleworking is combined with leave; leave shall not interrupt the consecutiveness. (ECB Staff Rules, page 84)

question: What is the minimum weekly working hour requirement for staff working part-time?
answer: The weekly working hours for part-time staff shall be at least 20 hours. The daily and weekly distribution of working hours shall be agreed between local management and the member of staff according to criteria established by the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy Director General. (ECB Staff Rules, page 88)

question: What is the maximum amount of overtime a staff member can be asked to work in a four-month period?
answer: The total overtime which a member of staff may be asked to work shall not exceed 136 hours in any four-month period. The combined daily working hours, including flexitime and overtime, may be extended to a maximum of 13 hours in exceptional individual cases. (ECB Staff Rules, page 89)

question: How much annual leave can I carry over to the next calendar year?
answer: Members of staff are entitled every year to carry over a maximum of 10.5 days of the annual leave entitlement. Those who, for business or personal reasons, have not taken their minimum of 20 days by the end of the calendar year may, on approval by their Director General/Director, take them until the end of March of the subsequent year. (ECB Staff Rules, page 93)

question: How many days of special leave do I receive for the birth of my child if I am not entitled to maternity leave?
answer: Special leave of 20 days per child is granted for the birth of a member of staff’s legitimate or natural child, provided that the member of staff is not entitled to maternity leave. (ECB Staff Rules, page 96)

question: How many days of parental special leave am I entitled to per dependent child, and within what timeframe must I use them?
answer: A member of staff on a fixed-term or permanent contract who has passed their probationary period and has completed at least one year of service is entitled to parental special leave of 40 working days for each dependent child. Parental special leave may only be used in the first three years after the birth of the child or the date of adoption. (ECB Staff Rules, page 99)

question: What is the maximum total duration of unpaid parental leave I can take across all my children?
answer: Unpaid parental leave shall be granted for a maximum of three years per child, which may be non-consecutive. The total unpaid parental leave granted to a member of staff shall not exceed six years. Notwithstanding this, two additional months of unpaid parental leave may be granted beyond this limit for each new dependent child. (ECB Staff Rules, page 100)

question: When is a medical certificate required for sick leave?
answer: A medical certificate is required when the absence is longer than two consecutive working days per period of absence; when a member of staff is unable to work the contractually agreed hours due to medical reasons; when unable to attend a disciplinary or underperformance hearing; when illness or accident occurs during annual leave; or when uncertified absences exceed ten working days per annum. A single medical certificate can certify an absence of up to a maximum of six weeks. (ECB Staff Rules, page 105)

question: Within what timeframe must an alleged accident at work be reported to the Directorate General Human Resources?
answer: The report shall be submitted not later than 10 working days following the date on which the alleged accident at work occurs, except where the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy has established that the delay was justified by force majeure or by any other legitimate reason. (ECB Staff Rules, page 112)
